[19:30] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "The Math Formula"
NÚMEROS: Financial goal ÷ Average customer value = Customer count needed
VISUAL: Equação matemática clara, setas mostrando processo reverso de engenharia
[19:32] And you need to be extremely clear on what that math is. And it shouldn't be like you're not solving a fucking physics problem, okay? You're doing pretty basic division and multiplication at most.
[19:45] Where you're like, "All right, here's the financial goal. Here's the average amount that people are spending with us." So, my financial goal divided by the average amount that I want people or that people currently are spending with us equals how many fucking customers I got to sell.
[20:03] It's like it all starts there. And then when you get the customer account, it's like then you take that customer count and you reverse engineer it into your acquisition math.

[20:43] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Reverse Engineering Example"
NÚMEROS: 100 customers needed, 40% close rate, 400 calls needed
VISUAL: Whiteboard com cálculos, setas regressivas: Goal → Customers → Calls → Leads → Ad Spend
[20:45] Okay. And just as an example like look on the board here for what I'm specifically showing. Okay. So, it started off with just again the number of customers that you need.
[21:00] Okay. And when you got the number of customers that you need, all you do is just reverse engineer. So you go into a financial model or you just do basic math like what's on the board with a calculator and you just work your way backwards.
[21:15] Where you're like, "All right, so I need 100 customers to sign up in a month to hit a million dollars a month. What's my current close rate?" So if my current close rate is 40%, I need 400 calls.
[21:30] You know, it's like I'm sorry, it'd be a little less than 400 calls. That'd be a 25% close rate. My point is is like then I get the amount of calls I need.
[21:40] And it's like, okay, well, do I have a one call close? Do I have a two call close? So, I have the show rate, the second call booking rate, the first call show rate, my cost per call, I'm just working my way backwards in the math.

[22:25] And in real time, we're just working our way back on the goals. That's it. It's just a real time equation.
[22:33] So it's like we know the math that we have to hit. We know the math ahead of time going into the month that we have to hit and then in real time we are either hitting the math or we are not hitting the math and making improvements to hit the math.

Because the math after you've connected the dots, right, after you've officially laid it out for yourself and you've gotten the clarity of being able to say, "Okay, I'm right here."
[23:50] And that the math is the bridge. The math is the clarity where it's like, I can see clear as day what I need to do to get to the other side.
[24:00] Or the math exposes that your current bridge isn't even possible to get built or it's going to take too much to build and therefore you make adjustments to your plan and you move accordingly.

[52:08] And Judge Graham sold two agencies one the first one was for 54 million and the second one was for 110 million.
[52:18] And Judge has since bought an insurance company and makes fucking buku bucks and his best friend cash flows $100 million a year with his business.

[1:15:45] All the advice you've probably gotten, just try to hit a just try to get on first baseman. You don't need to swing for the fences every time you go up to the plate. Every pitch you do not need to swing a home run.
[1:16:00] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Ohtani Didn't Listen"
NÚMEROS: $700M contract result
VISUAL: Rebeldia produtiva: ignorar conselhos limitantes = recompensa massiva
[1:16:03] Well, Ohtani, I mean, he didn't fucking listen to that shit. Quite the opposite. As a matter of fact, I'm sure as every fucking swing he took as a kid, he would probably flick the coach off and be like, "Give a fuck coach. I'm trying to hit home runs."
[1:16:20] All right. And not only did that clearly play out for him, he gets paid 700 fucking million dollars in his 10-year contract now.

[1:22:05] So, it's like the risk is therefore easy to take. You see, it's a big risk and it's uncomfortable, but it's like perceptually still probable to play out in our favor.
[1:22:18] Even if we aren't profitable on the front end, it's easy to take versus again when you look at things from a perspective of like risks where there's not as much certainty or risks where you know it's just there's less probability like the probabilities aren't as in your favor.

📊 RESUMO EXECUTIVO
NÚMEROS PRINCIPAIS:
$1M/month = Novo piso, não teto
$100K/month = Choraria se fizesse apenas isso
$42M/year → $100M goal (Jeremy Miner)
2.5x growth em 5 anos = altamente provável
$700M contract (Shohei Ohtani)
50-50 record (50 home runs + 50 stolen bases) - primeiro na história
$200K-$400K/month em ad spend típico para negócios de $1M/month
$35K/day → $75K/day ad spend (Cody Sanchez)
Mid couple hundred K net em $1M/month gross
$20K-$50K scholarships (Jack's business)
6-10 amigos morreram de heroína por perder propósito
1 em 8 pessoas na Terra trabalham em vendas
$54M primeira agência (Judge Graham)
$110M segunda agência (Judge Graham)
$100M/year cash flow (amigo de Judge)
1 point something % rev-share (Grant Cardone deal)
4 anos funcionário leal mas não evoluindo
40 empresas compradas por Dan Lok
200+ staff contratados (B&C players)
100 clientes necessários para $1M/month (exemplo matemático)
40% close rate exemplo
25% close rate exemplo alternativo
ESTRATÉGIAS E SISTEMAS:
1. CERTAINTY (Certeza)
Operar com 100% de certeza na missão
Rejeitar "hippie beliefs" limitantes
Não separar identidade interna (ego vs self)
Evitar humildade (definição = fraqueza)
Manter confiança inabalável no propósito
2. RIPPLE EFFECT THINKING (Pensamento de Efeito Cascata)
Ver impacto de longo prazo de cada ação
Jack's scholarships → salvam vidas, criam médicos, engenheiros
Economia de $20K-$50K → paga cirurgias futuras dos pais
Atletas inspiram crianças → previnem drogas e mortes
Pensar em cadeias longas de causa-efeito
3. THINK BIG BUT FEEL SMALL
Visão grande do impacto global
Sentir-se pequeno diante do que falta fazer
$1M/month = novo piso, não celebração
Novos objetivos: $2.5M-$10M/month
Nunca se sentir "chegado"
4. MATH-BASED GOALS (Objetivos Baseados em Matemática)
Objetivo financeiro ÷ ticket médio = clientes necessários
Clientes ÷ close rate = calls necessárias
Reverse engineering completo do funil
Financial model em tempo real
Math → Execute → Adjust → Repeat
5. A PLAYERS ONLY (Apenas Jogadores Elite)
Equipes pequenas de talento extremo
Navy SEALs, não exército regular
4-6 pessoas de elite > 1000 pessoas medianas
Referral network para encontrar talento
Swap players quando atingir teto
6. EXTREME ACCOUNTABILITY
Mentores que exigem crescimento
Não celebrar falhas de atingir meta
1-2 pessoas que venderam empresas por $100M+
Perspectiva que torna problemas triviais
2-5 minutos de conversas de alto impacto
7. DATA FILTRATION (Filtração de Dados)
Information vacuum + extreme filter
Rejeitar ativamente dados prejudiciais
Manter worldview otimizado para crescimento
Selecionar perspectivas recebidas
Não deixar crenças limitantes "grudar"
8. BIGGER MENTORS
Pessoas muito acima do seu nível
Judge Graham: $54M + $110M exits
Mentores que fazem problemas parecerem fáceis
Transição de mentores conforme cresce
Network de bilionários (caso Tai Lopez)
9. IT'S ALL A GAME
Manter diversão no processo
Evitar stress excessivo com números
Smile on face durante crescimento
Purpose-driven = happiness
Strategic worldview previne burnout
10. TAKE BIG SWINGS EVERY DAY
Shohei Ohtani: 100% home run swings
Nunca tentar apenas "base hits"
$35K → $75K/day ad spend casual
Balls on the table diariamente
Descondicionar "don't swing for fences"
ESTRUTURA:
CARACTERÍSTICAS DE MILLION-DOLLAR MONTH EARNERS:
Certainty (Certeza absoluta)
Purpose & Mission (Propósito e missão clara)
Demand Growth From Self (Exigem crescimento de si mesmos)
Think Big, Feel Small (Pensam grande, sentem-se pequenos)
Math-Based Realistic Goals (Objetivos realistas baseados em matemática)
The Right People (As pessoas certas - A players only)
Extreme Accountability (Accountability extrema)
Data Filtration (Filtração de dados)
Bigger Mentors (Mentores maiores)
It's All A Game (É tudo um jogo)
Take Big Swings (Tomam grandes riscos)
PROCESSO DE CRESCIMENTO:
Start with math
Reverse engineer funnel
Execute daily
Track in real-time
Adjust based on data
Never celebrate until surpassing goal
Immediately set new higher floor
Repeat
RED FLAGS QUE IMPEDEM CRESCIMENTO:
Celebrar antes de atingir meta
Dar dia de folga após primeira vitória
Pessoas B&C na equipe
Falta de math clarity
Tentar apenas "base hits"
Ouvir conselhos de pessoas sem resultados
Humildade mal-entendida
Separar ego de identidade
Não ter mentores maiores
Stress excessivo sem perspectiva de jogo
DECISÕES DIFÍCEIS NECESSÁRIAS:
Cortar funcionários leais que não evoluem
Swap players ao atingir teto
Gastar desconfortavelmente em talento
Tomar riscos que parecem irresponsáveis
Ignorar conselhos de pessoas menores
Não desacelerar por problemas de fulfillment
Shift beliefs da equipe ou cortar pessoas
DADOS FINANCEIROS:
INVESTIMENTOS TÍPICOS:
$200K-$400K/month em ad spend
A players: pequena rev-share (1-2%)
High payroll para talento elite
Mid couple hundred K net em $1M gross
ESTRUTURA DE RECEITA:
Front-end: pode ser break-even ou pequeno lucro
Back-end: onde está a real lucratividade
Ticket médio determina clientes necessários
Close rate determina calls necessárias
Cost per call determina budget de ads
ROI PERSPECTIVE:
Profitable by "handful of dollars" = acceptable
Long-term event profitability > short-term losses
Cash reserves permitem riscos calculados
2.5x growth em 5 anos = "measly"
$100K/month = "would cry"
COMPENSATION MODELS:
Rev-share para motivar A players
Equity/points em digital revenue
Performance-based incentives
Não precisa muito para pessoas certas
Overpay comparado a B players
DURAÇÃO: 1:37:48 FORMATO: Masterclass ao vivo / Consulting call recording
